title: Scheduling vs booking vs calendar view vs date picker
summary: Use scheduling for arranging a time plan with attendees, availability, recurrence, reminders, and conflicts; booking for reserving a service/resource slot; calendar view for inspecting scheduled items; and date picker for choosing a single date field.
decisionRules: Choose scheduling when the task is coordinating a meeting, event, availability schedule, or recurring time plan across people, calendars, time zones, reminders, locations, rooms, conferencing, buffers, limits, or free/busy conflicts.
Choose booking when the primary job is reserving a service, appointment, inventory, room, seat, or resource slot with customer-facing confirmation, capacity, payment, cancellation, or provider rules.
Choose calendar view when users need to inspect existing events spatially by day, week, month, resource, duration, overlap, or availability rather than create the scheduling contract itself.
Choose date picker when the user needs one date value and does not need attendee availability, recurrence, reminders, rooms, conferencing, buffers, or conflict handling.
Choose time picker when the user is choosing a clock time field from predictable increments inside a larger scheduling or booking flow.
Choose date range picker when users need a start and end date span; scheduling may use a range but also requires participants, availability, recurrence, timezone, and conflict behavior.
Use notification center to surface schedule updates, invitations, reminders, acceptances, declines, and cancellations, but keep schedule editing in the scheduling workflow.
Use conflict state when scheduling is blocked by attendee busy time, resource unavailability, invalid recurrence, hidden calendars, timezone ambiguity, or stale availability.

Scheduling must expose who or what is being scheduled, duration, start/end, timezone, recurrence, attendee response state, availability source, reminders, location or conference link, and save/send consequences.
Recurring scheduling must show series scope, recurrence rule, exceptions, end condition, timezone, conflict summary, and whether edits affect one occurrence or the full series.
failureModes: A meeting is scheduled without showing that a required attendee or room is busy.
A recurring series expands in the wrong local time because the timezone was hidden or changed silently.
The interface saves a draft event but users believe invitations were sent.
A schedule page shows free slots without accounting for buffers, maximum meetings per day, minimum notice, or checked calendars.
A date picker is used for a meeting with attendees, so users cannot see free/busy conflicts or send invitations.
Rescheduling one occurrence accidentally changes every recurrence in the series.
A reminder or video link is missing from the sent event even though it appeared in the draft.

sources.note: Google Calendar documents creating events with calendar selection, guests, rooms, location, conferencing, attachments, and save behavior.
Google Calendar documents availability, buffers, maximum bookings per day, guest permissions, selected calendars, and conflict checks for appointment schedules.
Outlook documents Scheduling Assistant free/busy views, recurrence, suggested times, and manual start/end scheduling.
Outlook on the web documents free/busy Scheduling Assistant, recurring meetings, save or send, and different time zone usefulness.
Calendly documents buffers, limits, free/busy rules, start increments, notice periods, date ranges, and checking the booking page.
Calendly documents event time zone settings, local time detection, user-selectable time zones, and locked event time zones.
Google Calendar API documents recurring event timezone requirements for expanding recurrences correctly.
